title: Castle in the Air - Chapter Two: In which Abdullah is mistaken for a young lady (Fragment 3)
text:
“Not in the least,” said Abdullah.

“I thought not,” she said, and looked up at him with great concern. “You do not seem to me to be a beast. This makes me quite sure that you can’t really be a man.” Evidently she was one of those people who like to cling to a theory once they have made it. After considering a moment, she asked, “Could your family, perhaps, for reasons of their own, have brought you up to believe a falsehood?”

Abdullah would have liked to say that the boot was on the other foot, but since that struck him as impolite, he simply shook his head and thought how generous of her it was to be so worried about him and how the worry on her face only made it more beautiful—not to speak of the way her eyes shone compassionately in the gold and silver light reflecting from the fountain.

“Perhaps it has something to do with the fact that you are from a distant country,” she said, and patted the edge of the fountain beside her. “Sit down and tell me all about it.”

“Tell me your name first,” said Abdullah.

“It’s rather a silly name,” she said nervously. “I’m called Flower-in-the-Night.”

It was the perfect name for the girl of his dreams, Abdullah thought. He gazed down at her admiringly. “My name is Abdullah,” he said.

“They even gave you a man’s name!” Flower-in-the-Night exclaimed indignantly. “Do sit down and tell me.”

Abdullah sat on the marble curb beside her and thought that this was a very real dream. The stone was cold. Splashes from the fountain soaked into his nightshirt, while the sweet smell of rose water from Flower-in-the-Night mingled most realistically with scents from the flowers in the garden. But since it was a dream, it followed that his daydreams were true here, too. So Abdullah told her all about the palace he had lived in as a prince and how he was kidnapped by Kabul Aqba and escaped into the desert, where the carpet merchant found him.

Flower-in-the-Night listened with complete sympathy. “How terrifying! How exhausting!” she said. “Could it be that your foster father was in league with the bandits to deceive you?”

Abdullah had a growing feeling, despite the fact that he was only dreaming, that he was getting her sympathy on false pretenses. He agreed that his father could have been in the pay of Kabul Aqba and then changed the subject. “Let us get back to your father and his plans,” he said. “It seems to me a little awkward that you should marry this Prince from Ochinstan without having seen any other men to compare him with. How are you going to know whether you love him or not?”

“You have a point,” she said. “This worries me, too, sometimes.”

“Then I tell you what,” Abdullah said. “Suppose I come back tomorrow night and bring you pictures of as many men as I can find? That should give you some standard to compare the Prince with.” Dream or not, Abdullah had absolutely no doubt that he would be back tomorrow. This would give him a proper excuse.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Two: In which Abdullah is mistaken for a young lady

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows respect and warm approval
vocabulary.example: He gazed down at her admiringly.

vocabulary.definition: very bad or unpleasant
vocabulary.example: I felt an awful mistake had been made.

vocabulary.definition: causing difficulty; hard to deal with
vocabulary.example: It seems to me a little awkward that you should marry this Prince.

vocabulary.definition: to hold on tightly to something, or to continue to believe something
vocabulary.example: People like to cling to a theory once they have made it.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows sympathy and concern for others
vocabulary.example: Her eyes shone compassionately in the gold and silver light.

vocabulary.definition: a raised edge or border, especially one dividing a street from a pavement
vocabulary.example: Abdullah sat on the marble curb beside her.

vocabulary.definition: got away from a place or dangerous situation
vocabulary.example: He escaped into the desert.

vocabulary.definition: said something suddenly and loudly from strong emotion
vocabulary.example: Flower-in-the-Night exclaimed indignantly.

vocabulary.definition: causing one to feel very tired
vocabulary.example: How exhausting!

vocabulary.definition: a statement that is not true; a lie
vocabulary.example: Have you brought yourself up to believe a falsehood?

vocabulary.definition: a man who is not a biological father but takes care of a child
vocabulary.example: Could it be that your foster father was in league with bandits?

vocabulary.definition: to look steadily and intently
vocabulary.example: He gazed down at her admiringly.

vocabulary.definition: in agreement or partnership, typically for dishonest or illegal purposes
vocabulary.example: His foster father was in league with the bandits.

vocabulary.definition: in a manner showing anger or annoyance at what is perceived as unfair treatment
vocabulary.example: Flower-in-the-Night exclaimed indignantly.

vocabulary.definition: taken away illegally by force or deception
vocabulary.example: He was kidnapped by Kabul Aqba.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows mercy or kindness
vocabulary.example: The carpet merchant found him mercifully.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that shows worry or anxiety
vocabulary.example: She said nervously.

vocabulary.definition: to lightly touch someone or something repeatedly with your hand flat
vocabulary.example: She patted the edge of the fountain.

vocabulary.definition: false reasons or excuses
vocabulary.example: He was getting her sympathy on false pretenses.

vocabulary.definition: throwing back light, heat, sound, or an image from a surface
vocabulary.example: Light reflecting from the fountain.

vocabulary.definition: to make something very wet by immersing or exposure to liquid
vocabulary.example: Splashes soaked into his nightshirt.

vocabulary.definition: small drops of liquid thrown outwards in various directions
vocabulary.example: Splashes from the fountain soaked into his nightshirt.

vocabulary.definition: a level of quality or attainment used as a measure
vocabulary.example: That should give you some standard to compare the Prince with.

vocabulary.definition: feelings of pity and sorrow for someone else's misfortune
vocabulary.example: Flower-in-the-Night listened with complete sympathy.

vocabulary.definition: causing extreme fear
vocabulary.example: How terrifying!
